section.title: The Work Resumes
section.sectionRationale: After years of standstill, the word of God through the prophets Haggai and Zechariah rouses the leaders to build again. When the Persian governor Tattenai challenges them, the elders keep working under the watchful eye of their God, give a bold and humble confession of their history, and the matter is referred to Darius. It is one movement: the prophetic word revives the work, and God's providence protects it.
section.thesis: God restarts His stalled work through the preaching of His prophets, and He guards it by His watchful eye, so that even an official inquiry cannot halt the builders whose confidence rests not in their own permits but in the God of heaven.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the prophets stir the leaders and the building resumes, verses 1 and 2; Tattenai's challenge met under God's watchful eye, verses 3 to 5; and the elders' confession quoted in Tattenai's report to Darius, verses 6 to 17.

units.label: The prophets stir the work to resume
units.note: the prophets of God were with them

verses.text: Later, the prophets Haggai and Zechariah son of Iddo {{neba|prophesied}} to the Jews in Judah and Jerusalem in the name of the God of Israel, who was over them.
Then Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel and Jeshua son of Jozadak rose up and began to rebuild the house of God in Jerusalem. And the prophets of God were with them, helping them.

units.label: The challenge met under God's watchful eye
units.note: the eye of their God was on the elders

verses.text: At that time Tattenai the governor of the region west of the Euphrates, Shethar-bozenai, and their associates went to the Jews and asked, “Who authorized you to rebuild this temple and restore this structure?”
They also asked, “What are the names of the men who are constructing this building?”
But the {{ayin|eye}} of their God was on the elders of the Jews, so that they were not stopped until a report was sent to Darius and written instructions about this matter were returned.

units.label: The elders' confession in Tattenai's report
units.note: we are servants of the God of heaven and earth

verses.text: This is the text of the letter that Tattenai the governor of the region west of the Euphrates, Shethar-bozenai, and their associates, the officials in the region, sent to King Darius.
The report they sent him read as follows: To King Darius: All peace.

Let it be known to the king that we went into the province of Judah, to the house of the great God. The people are rebuilding it with large stones and placing timbers in the walls. This work is being carried out {{osparna|diligently}} and is prospering in their hands.
So we questioned the elders and asked, “Who authorized you to rebuild this temple and restore this structure?”
We also asked for their names, so that we could write down the names of their leaders for your information.

And this is the answer they returned: “We are servants of the {{elah-shemaya|God of heaven}} and earth, and we are rebuilding the temple that was built many years ago, which a great king of Israel built and completed.
But since our fathers angered the God of heaven, He delivered them into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, the Chaldean who destroyed this temple and carried away the people to Babylon.
In the first year of his reign, however, Cyrus king of Babylon issued a decree to rebuild this house of God.

He also removed from the temple of Babylon the gold and silver articles belonging to the house of God, which Nebuchadnezzar had taken and carried there from the temple in Jerusalem. King Cyrus gave these articles to a man named Sheshbazzar, whom he appointed governor
and instructed, ‘Take these articles, put them in the temple in Jerusalem, and let the house of God be rebuilt on its original site.’
So this Sheshbazzar came and laid the foundation of the house of God in Jerusalem, and from that time until now it has been under construction, but it has not yet been completed.”

Now, therefore, if it pleases the king, let a search be made of the royal archives in Babylon to see if King Cyrus did indeed issue a decree to rebuild the house of God in Jerusalem. Then let the king send us his decision in this matter.

neba.gloss: To prophesy, to speak God's word. The verb, from the Aramaic section, names the engine of the revival: the stalled work resumes not by new political permission but because Haggai and Zechariah prophesied. God's word, preached, is what raises His discouraged people to build again.

ayin.gloss: Eye. The phrase the eye of their God was on the elders pictures God's watchful, protective providence. His attentive care, not a royal permit, is why the officials could not stop the work while the appeal was pending; God was keeping His own.

osparna.gloss: Diligently, thoroughly, with care; a Persian loanword that recurs through the Aramaic documents of Ezra. Even a foreign governor reports that the work is being done osparna, and later the same word marks how carefully the empire itself will fund and protect it, all under God's hand.

elah-shemaya.gloss: The God of heaven, a favorite title in the Aramaic chapters. Standing before an imperial governor, the elders confess themselves servants of the God of heaven and earth, staking their identity and their building on the sovereign Lord above every earthly power.

basic.context: The temple project had been stalled for years. Then God sends two prophets, Haggai and Zechariah, whose preaching stirs Zerubbabel and Jeshua to start building again. The regional governor Tattenai shows up and challenges their right to build, asking who authorized it and demanding names. But the work continues, because God's watchful eye is on the elders, and Tattenai agrees to let it proceed while he refers the question to King Darius. The elders answer boldly, confessing that they are servants of the God of heaven, that the exile was deserved judgment, and that Cyrus himself had decreed the rebuilding. The question is: what gets God's stalled work moving, and what keeps it safe? God's word and God's watchful care.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative that shows, not commands. It highlights how God revives His people, through the preaching of His word, and how He protects them, by His providence. The elders' honest confession models faithful witness. We should not expect governors and archives in our own day, but we should expect that God still works through His word and still watches over His people.
basic.originalLanguages: The revival begins because the prophets neba, prophesied. The reason the work is not stopped is that the ayin, the eye, of their God was on the elders. Even the governor admits the work is being done osparna, diligently. And the builders confess themselves servants of the elah shemaya, the God of heaven.
basic.typology: The prophetic word that raises up the builders points to the living word of Christ that builds His church. God's watchful eye over the elders points to the Good Shepherd who keeps His flock. And the confession of sin met by God's mercy points to the gospel itself.
basic.christ: God's stalled work came back to life through His preached word, and that is exactly how Christ builds His church, by the word of the gospel proclaimed. The watchful eye of God that shielded the builders is the same care Jesus promises His people, that no one can snatch them from His hand. And the elders' confession, owning their sin yet resting on God's mercy through Cyrus, is a small picture of the gospel: judgment deserved, grace given.
basic.god: God is the one who revives and protects. He raises up prophets to move His people, and He keeps His watchful eye on them so their enemies cannot stop the work before its time. He is the God of heaven and earth, sovereign over governors, kings, and archives alike.
basic.crossReferences: Haggai 1 and Zechariah 4 record the very preaching that restarted the work. Zechariah 4:6 says it is not by might but by God's Spirit. And 1 Peter 3:12 echoes that the eyes of the Lord are on the righteous.
basic.oneStory: God's work stalls, and then His word, through His prophets, brings it back to life, while His watchful eye guards it from being stopped. This is how God has always advanced His purposes, and how He builds Christ's church still: by the power of His word and the shelter of His providence.

context.literary: The chapter turns the narrative from delay to renewal, framed by the ministry of the prophets at the start and the appeal to Darius at the end. Its heart is verse 5, the eye of their God, a theological aside that interprets the whole scene. The elders' confession is preserved inside Tattenai's official report, a document-within-a-document typical of the Aramaic section and lending the account an air of archival authenticity.
context.historical: Haggai and Zechariah preached in the second year of Darius, around 520 BC, dating the resumption precisely. Tattenai was the Persian governor of the vast satrapy west of the Euphrates, and his inquiry followed proper imperial procedure: verify authorization, record the responsible parties, and refer the matter upward. His willingness to let the work continue pending the king's ruling was itself a mercy of God.
context.canonical: This chapter is the narrative counterpart to the books of Haggai and Zechariah, showing the effect of their preaching. It advances the Bible's theme that God's word is the power that moves His people, and that His providence quietly overrules empires. The confession of the elders, honest about sin yet hopeful in God's mercy, fits the pattern of restoration that runs toward the gospel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the prophetic word of God revived the stalled rebuilding, and that God's watchful providence protected His people through the governor's inquiry, so that the reader trusts God's word to move and God's eye to guard His work.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical resumption and an imperial legal process; it does not prescribe a method for handling permits. What endures is the truth that God works through His preached word to stir His people, that He watches over them providentially, and that faithful witnesses can confess both their sin and their hope honestly before the world.

debates.question: What truly got the work moving again after years of standstill?

views.label: The prophetic word of God
views.case: The chapter credits the resumption to Haggai and Zechariah prophesying; the parallel books show their preaching directly rebuking apathy and promising God's presence, so the primary cause is God's word working on the people's hearts.

views.label: The renewed resolve of the leaders
views.case: Zerubbabel and Jeshua rose up and began to build; human courage and leadership were the visible means by which the work advanced, showing that God's word bears fruit through responsible action.

debates.bearing: Both are true and ordered: God's word through the prophets stirred the leaders, who then acted, teaching that revival comes as God's word moves His people to obedient effort.

debates.question: Why does the elders' answer dwell on their fathers' sin and the exile?

views.label: Honest covenant confession
views.case: They freely admit that their fathers angered the God of heaven and were justly handed over to Babylon; the temple's ruin was deserved judgment, so its rebuilding is sheer mercy, and the confession magnifies God's grace rather than Israel's merit.

views.label: A truthful legal defense
views.case: By recounting the history and citing Cyrus's decree, the elders establish the legitimacy of their building under Persian law; the confession doubles as a documented case that the governor can verify in the royal archives.

debates.bearing: Either way, the answer models faithful witness: it tells the truth about sin and judgment while resting the whole hope of the rebuilt house on the God of heaven and His mercy.

study.originalLanguages: Written in Aramaic, the chapter states its theology in a few key words. The turning point is that the prophets neba, prophesied: the stalled work revives because God speaks, and His word, preached, is the power that raises a discouraged people. The reason the officials cannot stop them is that the ayin, the eye, of their God was on the elders, a vivid picture of watchful, protecting providence. Even the hostile-leaning governor reports that the building goes forward osparna, diligently and thoroughly, a recurring word in Ezra that will soon describe how the empire itself carefully funds the work. And at the center stands the builders' confession: they are servants of the elah shemaya, the God of heaven and earth, staking everything on the sovereign Lord who stands above Tattenai, above Darius, and above every archive in Babylon.

typology.type: The prophetic word that raises up the builders
typology.fulfillment: The living and abiding word of Christ by which He builds His church, 1 Peter 1:23-25 and Romans 10:17.

typology.type: The eye of God guarding the elders
typology.fulfillment: The Good Shepherd's keeping of His flock, from whose hand no one can snatch them, John 10:28-29.

typology.type: Sin confessed and met by God's mercy
typology.fulfillment: The gospel, in which deserved judgment gives way to grace through Christ, Romans 3:23-24.

study.christ: The dead-stopped work sprang back to life the moment God's word was preached, and that is precisely how Christ builds His church, not by human permits or political might but by the proclaimed word of the gospel that gives new birth. The eye of God that shielded the builders from being stopped is the same watchful care Jesus promises His people, holding them so securely that no enemy can pluck them from His hand. And the elders' confession is quietly gospel-shaped: they own that their fathers deserved the exile, yet the very existence of the rebuilt temple testifies to mercy. So the good news runs, judgment justly fell, but grace, at last in Christ, has the final word.
study.god: God is the reviver and the keeper of His work. He raises up prophets to stir a discouraged people, showing that the power to move hearts belongs to His word, not to circumstances. He fixes His watchful eye on the elders so that all the authority of the empire cannot halt them before His appointed time. And He is confessed for who He is, the God of heaven and earth, sovereign over Cyrus, Nebuchadnezzar, Tattenai, and Darius alike, ruling the rise and fall of kingdoms to build His house. This is a God worth serving before any earthly power.

crossReferences.note: The people obey the LORD through Haggai's preaching and resume work on the house, the event behind Ezra 5:1-2.
Not by might nor by power but by My Spirit; Zerubbabel's hands will finish the temple, the promise fulfilled in these chapters.
The eye of the LORD is on those who fear Him, the providence pictured in Ezra 5:5.
Christ's sheep are held so securely that no one can snatch them from His hand, the reality the guarded builders foreshadow.

study.oneStory: God's work had ground to a halt, and then His word, through the prophets, raised it up again, while His watchful eye kept it safe from every attempt to stop it. This is the enduring pattern of God's dealings: His word moves His people, and His providence protects them. It runs straight to Christ, who builds His church by the preached gospel and keeps His people by His unbreakable care, until the house of God is finished at last.

quiz.q: What caused the stalled building work to resume?

options.text: The preaching of the prophets Haggai and Zechariah, which stirred the leaders to build.
options.note: Verses 1-2 credit the resumption to the prophesying of Haggai and Zechariah.

options.text: A new army sent to protect the builders.
options.note: No army appears; it was the prophetic word that moved them.

options.text: The death of all their enemies.
options.note: The enemies were still active, as Tattenai's inquiry shows.

options.text: A fresh decree from Tattenai the governor.
options.note: Tattenai questioned the work; he did not command it to resume.

quiz.q: Why were the builders not stopped when Tattenai questioned them?

options.text: Because the eye of their God was on the elders, guarding the work.
options.note: Verse 5 attributes their protection to God's watchful providence.

options.text: Because they bribed the governor to leave them alone.
options.note: No bribe is mentioned; God's watchful care is the stated reason.

options.text: Because they had a large army to resist him.
options.note: Their security was God's eye upon them, not military strength.

options.text: Because Tattenai supported their cause from the start.
options.note: Tattenai challenged them and referred the matter to Darius.

quiz.q: How did the elders describe themselves and their history to Tattenai?

options.text: As servants of the God of heaven, whose fathers had sinned and been exiled, now rebuilding by Cyrus's decree.
options.note: Verses 11-16 give their honest confession of sin and appeal to Cyrus's decree.

options.text: As an independent nation owing Persia nothing.
options.note: They appealed to Cyrus's authorization, not independence.

options.text: As people who had never done anything wrong.
options.note: They openly confessed that their fathers had angered God.

options.text: As worshipers of the Persian gods.
options.note: They confessed the God of heaven and earth, not Persia's gods.

quiz.q: What did the elders confess about why the first temple was destroyed?

options.text: Their fathers angered the God of heaven, so He handed them over to Nebuchadnezzar.
options.note: Verse 12 owns the exile as God's just judgment on their fathers' sin.

options.text: Nebuchadnezzar was simply stronger than their God.
options.note: The opposite: God Himself delivered them into Nebuchadnezzar's hand.

options.text: It was an accident of history with no cause.
options.note: They name their fathers' sin as the cause, not chance.

options.text: The Persians had destroyed it.
options.note: Babylon under Nebuchadnezzar destroyed it, as they state.

quiz.q: How does the way the work resumed point us to Christ?

options.text: God revived His work by His preached word, as Christ builds His church by the gospel, and guards His people as the Good Shepherd.
options.note: The prophetic word and God's watchful eye foreshadow Christ's word and care.

options.text: It shows that God's work depends on government approval.
options.note: The work advanced under God's word and eye before any approval came.

options.text: It teaches that prophets are no longer needed at all.
options.note: The point is the power of God's word, now fully in Christ and Scripture.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The word that revives and the eye that guards both point to Christ.

questions: The stalled work revived when God's word was preached. Where in your life or church has a season of drift needed the fresh stirring of God's word, and how might you sit under it again?
The builders kept working because the eye of their God was on them. How does knowing that God is watching over you with protective care change the way you face opposition or scrutiny?
The elders confessed their fathers' sin openly, yet without despair, resting on God's mercy. How can you learn to be honest about sin while still being confident in grace?
Standing before a powerful governor, the elders identified themselves first as servants of the God of heaven. When your identity is tested by the powers around you, what do you lead with?
God overruled an imperial inquiry to protect His people's work. Where do you need to trust God's quiet providence over decisions and authorities beyond your control?

translationNotes.note: Haggai and Zechariah son of Iddo are the same prophets whose books date this preaching to the second year of Darius, around 520 BC; their message is preserved in Haggai 1 and Zechariah 1-8.
The eye of their God was on the elders renders an Aramaic idiom for watchful, favorable providence; it is the theological center of the chapter, explaining why the work was not stopped.
Diligently renders the Aramaic osparna, a Persian loanword recurring in Ezra's official documents; it stresses that the work, and later the empire's support of it, was carried out thoroughly.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Ezra 5:1-17, which lies wholly within the Aramaic section (4:8-6:18); key words are marked Aramaic. No em or en dashes present or introduced.
